elements.sent-2411-001: Understanding the complex ground state of van der Waals (vdW) magnets is essential for designing new materials and devices that leverage these platforms.
elements.sent-2411-002: Here, we investigate a two-dimensional vdW ferromagnet -- \FGT -- with one of the highest reported Curie temperatures, to elucidate its magnetic excitations and charge order.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using Fe $L_3 - $edge resonant inelastic x-ray scattering, we find the dual character of magnetic excitations, consisting of a coherent magnon and a continuum, similar to what is reported for its sister compound \FethGT.
elements.sent-2411-004: The magnon has an energy of $\approx$ 36 meV at the maximum in-plane momentum transfer ($-$0.35 r.l.u.) allowed at Fe $L_3 - $edge.
elements.sent-2411-005: A broad and non-dispersive continuum extends up to 150 meV, 50$\%$ higher energy than in \FethGT.
elements.sent-2411-006: Its intensity is sinusoidally modulated along the $L$ direction, with a period matching the inter-slab distance.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings suggest that while the unconventional dual character of magnetic excitations is generic to ternary \FeGeTe vdW magnets, the correlation length of the out-of-plane magnetic interaction increases in \FfGT as compared to \FethGT, supporting a stronger three-dimensional character for the former.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, by investigating the $\pm$(1/3, 1/3, $L$) peaks by resonant x-ray diffraction, we conclude these to have structural origin rather than charge order -- as previously reported -- and suggest doubling of the structural unit cell along the $c-$axis.
elements.sent-2411-009: The discovery of ferromagnetism in two-dimensional van der Waals ferromagnets have opened exciting frontiers in quantum matter research \cite{Burch2018MagnetismMaterials,Gibertini2019MagneticHeterostructures,Wang2021TheMaterials .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this context, the ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials, such as \FethGT and \FGT , hosting high-temperature long-range ferromagnetic order down to the \gls*{2D structural limit is a unique platform where miniaturized spintronics devices have already been proposed \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle,Zhang2020ItinerantTemperature,Chen2023Above-Room-Temperature2,Wang2018TunnelingHeterostructures,Georgopoulou-Kotsaki2023Significant,Li2018Patterning,Alghamdi2019Highly,Yang2020Highly,Ding2020Observtion,Wu2020Neel,Deng2018Gate-tunableFe3GeTe2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In addition, strong electronic correlations are reported to drive these systems towards exotic ground states, that is, the heavy Fermions/ Kondo physics~\cite{Yun2018Emergence,Bao2022Neutron2 , \gls*{CO ~\cite{Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 , and \gls*{OSMT ~\cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One of the intriguing findings is the coexistence of low energy coherent magnetic excitations (magnons) and a dispersionless non-Stoner type continuum at higher energies \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2,Bao2022Neutron2,Xu2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This dual character of magnetic excitations is beyond the framework of the linear spin wave theory, invoking the \gls*{OSMT scenario -- atypical to 3$d$ transition metals -- to explain the experimental observations \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Are these dual magnetic excitations, a generic feature of the ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials? If so, how these excitations evolve with $T_{\rm C}$? \FGT with the highest $T_{\rm{C \approx$ 315 K among \FeGeTe 2D-vdW ferromagnetic materials is an ideal member to investigate these questions.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreover, it hosts the long-range ferromagnetic order remarkably close to room temperature $\approx$ 270 K in the \gls*{2D structural limit \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle,Zhang2020ItinerantTemperature,Chen2023Above-Room-Temperature2,Georgopoulou-Kotsaki2023Significant .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Deciphering the mechanism behind these high temperature \gls*{2D vdW ferromagnets is essential to engineer novel heterostructures from \gls*{2D layers.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, despite enormous progress in understanding transport, magnetic and structural properties of \FGT \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle,Zhang2020ItinerantTemperature,Chen2023Above-Room-Temperature2 , to date, the character of the magnetic excitations, their energy scale, and dimensionality remain unexplored in this material.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Inelastic neutron scattering (INS) is a standard technique to address these issues \cite{Bettler2019MagneticPbVO,Bhartiya2021InelasticBaVO but the small size of the \FGT single crystals poses a challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, a recent \gls*{ARPES and \gls*{STM study reports a charge order ~\cite{Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 , that is shown to compete with the magnetic ordering.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, a bulk-sensitive signature of the \gls*{CO , its electronic character and propagation vector are unexplored so far.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Here we present an investigation of the magnetic excitations and charge order in \FfGT.
elements.sent-2411-022: To elucidate the character, energy scale, and dimensionality of the magnetic excitations, we employed high-resolution Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS on a mm sized single crystal \FfGT.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our findings suggests that \FGT hosts dual character of magnetic excitations, similarly to \FethGT, and despite being a \gls*{2D system structurally, it displays a significant three-dimensional (3D) interaction between \FeGeTe slabs that are $\sim$10 \AA \, apart.}
elements.sent-2411-024: To investigate the bulk and electronic character of the proposed \gls*{CO , we used \gls*{XRD and Fe $K - $edge \gls*{REXS ~\cite{Abbamonte2004CrystallizationSr14Cu24O41,Chen2016Remarkable4,Shen2021ChargeNickelates .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our results demonstrate that the observed $\pm$(1/3, 1/3, $L$) peaks are of structural origin, suggesting a doubling of structural unit cell along the $c$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-026: Overall, our work advances the current understanding of the magnetic properties of \FGT and shed light onto the whole family of \FeGeTe vdW magnets.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figures/fig1.pdf} \caption{Magnetic excitations in the Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS} spectra of \FfGT. (a) \FGT crystal structure drawn using VESTA \cite{VESTA}. A single unit cell contains three \FeGeTe slabs. (b) Schematic of \gls*{RIXS} experimental geometry. In the hexagonal structure representation, two orthogonal wave vectors -- (100) and (001) -- form the scattering plane highlighted in pink. $\theta$ is an angle between the incident x-ray and the (100) axis while $\Omega$ is the scattering angle between incoming and outgoing x-ray. The double headed arrow represents the $\pi$ polarization of the incident light. (c) Fe $L_{3, 2} - $edge XAS measured at $\theta = 15^\circ$ in total electron yield (TEY) mode at 70 K. A solid vertical line represents the Fe $L_3 - $edge maximum, that corresponds to the incident photon energy (707.9 eV) used for the \gls*{RIXS} spectra. (d) RIXS intensity map measured as a function of incident photon around the Fe $L_3 - $edge, and zoomed in to cover excitations below 300 meV. For this dataset, $\theta = 20^\circ$ and $\Omega = 95^\circ$ corresponding to $\bm{Q}$ = ($-0.16$ 0 2.17). (e-f). Two representative \gls*{RIXS} scans (black dotted line) at ($-0.35$ 0 1.4) and ($-0.3$ 0 1.74). The low-energy excitations are fitted with an elastic peak (gray), a phonon (blue), a magnon (red), a broad continuum, and a background (gray line) stretching to higher energies. The red solid line represents the fit sum. The inset in (e) shows the measured \FfGT single crystal. All RIXS spectra displayed in this work were measured at $T$ = 70 K, using $\pi$ polarization. The error bars of the \gls*{RIXS} spectra are defined assuming a Poisson distribution of the single-photon counted events.}\label{FIG:fig1}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig2.pdf} \caption{$L$ -- dependent study of the magnetic continuum in \FGT. (a-g) \gls*{RIXS} spectra measured at different $L$ values from 1.74 r.l.u. to 3.23 r.l.u., at 70 K. For all spectra, $H < |0.04|$ and $K$= 0. Each spectrum is fitted with an elastic peak (gray), a phonon (blue), a magnon (red), and a continuum (green), as introduced in Figs. \ref{FIG:fig1}(e, f). The vertical dashed line marks the continuum centers around $\sim$ 75 meV. (h) $L$-dependence of the continuum integrated spectral weight (green dotted line). The error-bars are extracted from the fitting through the error propagation method. The thick green line is a sinusoidal fit of the data using Eq. (\ref{eq:equation}) for $d$ = 10 \AA, while the dotted lines represent the upper and lower boundaries considering the extracted fit error for $d$ of $\pm$ 1 \AA. (i) Calculated intensity modulation for intra-slab (red) and inter-slab (black) distances, as defined within the crystal structure displayed in (j) using with the same color convention. A gold curved line in (j) highlights the Te -- Te distance across two neighboring slabs.} \label{FIG:ldep}
elements.sent-2411-027: Sample and experimental details The high quality quenched single crystal of Fe$_{4.75}$GeTe$_2$ used for this study was taken from the same batch as in Ref.~\cite{MayPRB2016_Fe3GT,May2019FerromagnetismArticle .}
elements.sent-2411-028: For simplicity, we refer to Fe$_{4.75}$GeTe$_2$ as \FGT throughout the text.
elements.sent-2411-029: Figure~\ref{FIG:fig1 (a) shows the rhombohedral unit cell of \FGT ($R\bar{3 m$ No. 166, a = b $\sim $ 4.04 \AA, \, c $\sim$ 29.19 \AA), characterized by three distinct \FeGeTe slabs separated by $\sim$11 \AA.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Within each slab, the Fe and Ge atoms occupy interior positions, while the Te atoms occupy the external positions.
elements.sent-2411-031: For the \gls*{XAS and \gls*{RIXS studies, the \FGT single crystal was oriented with (1 0 0) and (0 0 1) in the scattering plane, see Fig. \ref{FIG:fig1 (b).}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The sample was cleaved in air with scotch tape just before loading it into the vacuum chamber.
elements.sent-2411-033: Figure \ref{FIG:fig1 (c) shows the \gls*{XAS measured in the \gls*{TEY mode at 70 K.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The observed $L_3$ and $L_2$ absorption peaks are in agreement with literature \cite{Yamagami2022EnhancedFe5GT , supporting the high quality of the sample .The \gls*{XAS data was used to identify the resonant energy for the \gls*{RIXS study.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: To reduce the elastic scattering signal in the RIXS measurements, we used $\pi$ polarized incoming x-rays.
elements.sent-2411-036: The same sample was used for both \gls*{REXS and \gls*{XRD experiments, and it was aligned using an out-of-plane and two in-plane nuclear reflections.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The momentum transfer $\bm{Q (H K L)$ associated with the RIXS spectra and the diffraction data are expressed in reciprocal lattice units ($4\pi/\sqrt{3 a$ $4\pi/\sqrt{3 a$ $2\pi/c$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The in-plane and out-of-plane momentum coverage was achieved by rotating both the scattering angle $\Omega$ and the x-rays incident angle $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Extensive momentum coverage was achieved for the \gls*{XRD experiment (2 keV of photon energy) and for the Fe $K - $edge \gls*{REXS ($\sim$7 keV of photon energy).}}
elements.sent-2411-040: For Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS ($\sim$710 eV of photon energy), we could span a large portion of the $\bm{Q}$-space in the $L$ direction across several \gls*{BZ and up to $L$ = 3.23 $(2\pi/c)$, thanks to the large $c$-axis ($\sim$ 29.19 \AA) of the \FGT, while in-plane we could cover up to $H_{max = -0.35$ $(4\pi/\sqrt{3 a)$ within the first BZ.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In the following two sections we describe the investigation of the magnetic excitations and their momentum dependence in \FGT using Fe $L_3 - $edge RIXS, and we discuss the results in comparison \FethGT \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2, Calder2019, Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Figure~\ref{FIG:fig1 (d) presents a \gls*{RIXS intensity map measured as a function of incident photon energy across the Fe $L_3 - $edge.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Although most of the spectral weight goes into high-energy fluorescence as expected for a metal (see Supplementary Fig. S3.1) \cite{Pelliciari2021TuningConfinement , a weak component stretches from 0 up to $\sim$ 150 meV resonating at the Fe $L_3$ peak.}
elements.sent-2411-045: By fixing the incident photon energy to the maximum of the Fe $L_3$ resonance, we measured high-resolution RIXS spectra as a function of momentum transfer $\bm{Q}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Figures \ref{FIG:fig1 (e,f) show \gls*{RIXS spectra at two representative $\bm{Q}$ points with largest in-plane momentum components, $\bm{Q}$ = ($-0.35$ 0 1.4) and ($-0.3$ 0 1.74).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We make use of both \gls*{RIXS spectra to establish a minimum fitting model that describes the observed inelastic spectral weight below 200 meV.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Details regarding the fitting and its procedure are reported in Supplementary Note S3.
elements.sent-2411-049: Taking \FethGT as a reference case \cite{Bao2022Neutron2,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 , we include in the fitting model a resolution limited phonon (blue peak), a magnon mode (red peak) due to spin-spin correlations between localized spins, and a broad magnetic continuum (green peak) coming from spin-spin interactions among itinerant electrons.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We fix the phonon energy to 20 meV based on the room temperature Raman measurements presented in Supplementary Fig. S1.1 and consistently with \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: From the remaining spectral weight, we extract a magnon peak centered at 36 meV $\pm 1$~meV and a continuum centered at 75 meV $\pm 2$~meV, extending up to $\sim$ 150 meV.
elements.sent-2411-052: At higher energies, a background from the fluorescence tail dominates the spectral weight (gray solid line).
elements.sent-2411-053: For the spectra presented in Figs.~\ref{FIG:fig1 (e,\,f), we were not able to resolve any significant magnon dispersion given the proximity of the in-plane $\bm{Q}$ components.}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, a comparable magnon energy for similar momentum transfer has been reported for \FethGT \cite{Calder2019,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2,Bao2022Neutron2 , further supporting the nature of the $\sim$ 36 meV peak.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Similarly, the broad continuum peak (stretching up to 150 meV) resembles the one identified in \FethGT \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 , although in the latter system it extends only up to 100 meV.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Based on these findings, we identify the low energy magnetic excitations in \FGT, and we confirm the coexistence of the dual electron spin character - localized and itinerant - similarly to \FethGT.
elements.sent-2411-057: This result suggests that OSMT scenario should be considered also for \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-058: Figure \ref{FIG:ldep shows the $L$ dependent \gls*{RIXS spectra along (0 0 $L$) from $L$ = 1.74 r.l.u.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In these measurements, we maximized the out-of-plane coverage by scanning mostly along (0 0 $L$), while minimizing the in-plane momentum transfer, e.g. $H < |0.04|$ r.l.u.
elements.sent-2411-060: A clear continuum peak emerges from all spectra, see Figs.\,\ref{FIG:ldep (a-g), and its position remains mostly invariant versus $L$ as marked by a vertical dashed line.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The dispersionless nature of the continuum agrees with the \FethGT findings \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: To directly compare the absolute \gls*{RIXS intensities as a function of $L$ (or scattering geometries), all spectra reported here are self-absorption corrected as described in Supplementary Note S2.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Already from the raw data, the continuum intensity appears to decrease for decreasing $L$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Note that while in \FethGT the continuum is maximized around the in-plane \gls*{BZ boundary~\cite{Bao2022Neutron2 , here we find a significant spectral weight close to the in-plane \gls*{BZ center.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The origin of the $L$-dependent intensity modulation of the continuum in \FethGT was associated with the two Fe sites, yielding a characteristic length scale along the $c-$axis within \FeGeTe slab \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The goal of this section is to extract this information for \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-067: We fit the \gls*{RIXS spectra in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep using the model introduced in Figs.. \ref{FIG:fig1 (e,\,f).}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: To effectively implement the fitting model, we made some key assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-069: Given the strong elastic line intensity and the unknown magnon dispersion for this material, we fixed the fitting parameters (peak position and width) of the lowest energy modes - phonon and magnon - to match the values extracted for $\bm{Q}$ = ($-0.35$ 0 1.4) r.l.u.
elements.sent-2411-070: In this way, we could account for most of the low energy spectral weight (below 40 meV), without affecting the dispersionless continuum.
elements.sent-2411-071: Regarding the fitting of the continuum itself, we fixed its position and width to reproduce the broad and dispersionless character of this excitation \cite{Bao2022Neutron2,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 , while its amplitude was allowed to change.}
elements.sent-2411-072: More details on the fitting procedure can be found in Supplementary Note S3.
elements.sent-2411-073: As a result of this analysis, we obtain the $L$-dependent evolution of the continuum intensity which follows a sinusoidal behavior, as highlighted in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (h).}
elements.sent-2411-074: When dealing with a continuum or a dispersionless excitation, the $\bm{Q}$-dependent modulation of its intensity or structure factor encodes the distance between the interacting atoms along the selected $\bm{Q}$-direction.
elements.sent-2411-075: Hard x-ray RIXS and INS -- enabling a wide coverage of reciprocal space -- have been extensively used to show the formation of new magnetic superstructures by monitoring the $\bm{Q}$-modulation of the spectral weight ~\cite{Revelli2020FingerprintsIridates,Revelli2022Quasimolecular, Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Following these works, the frequency of the continuum structure factor modulation can be captured by a simple qualitative function: \begin{equation}\label{eq:equation} I(L) \propto \cos{(L \cdot {d}/2)}^2,\end{equation} where $L$ is the momentum transfer vector and ${d}$ is the real space inter-atomic distance along the $c-$axis within the unit cell.
elements.sent-2411-077: By fitting the data points displayed in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (h), we extract a distance of ${d}$ = $ 10 \pm 1$ \AA.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the case of \FethGT, the $L$-dependent INS structure factor of the continuum yielded an intra-slab distance ${d \sim 2.7$ \AA \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Such a length-scale corresponds to the Fe-Fe dumbbell (a dimer like structure corresponding to the largest exchange interaction) superstructure oriented along the $c-$axis within an individual \FeGeTe slab.
elements.sent-2411-080: This finding suggests that the magnetic continuum in \FethGT originates from the quasi-molecular character of the magnetic states formed by the Fe-Fe dumbbell, and furthermore it identifies the atoms and exchange path that contribute to the strongest magnetic exchange interaction in the system~\cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: To identify the interacting atoms and exchange path behind the continuum intensity modulation in \FGT, we display in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (i) a simulation of $I(L)$ based on a representative intra-slab distance (red), and inter-slab distance (black).}
elements.sent-2411-082: Such distances are highlighted in the unit cell of Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (l), using the same color code.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The intra-slab distance $d \sim 2.53$ \AA\, connecting two Fe atoms within the same \FeGeTe slab fails in reproducing the observed modulation, despite it well described the $L$-dependent continuum modulation for \FethGT \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Rather, we find that an inter-slab distance $d \sim 11$ \AA \, connecting the center of two consecutive slabs well reproduces the continuum modulation for \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our finding supports the presence of an inter-slab exchange path along the $c$-axis, suggesting a three-dimensional character for the magnetic interaction in \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-086: Additionally, we identify the \FeGeTe slabs as the interacting magnetic units in \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-087: This is consistent with recent \gls*{XMCD and \gls*{ARPES studies underlining the key role of Te and Ge in determining the itinerant long-range ferromagnetism in \FGT \cite{Yamagami2022EnhancedFe5GT, Yamagami2021giant , owing to the strong hybridization and covalent nature of the Fe-Te and the Fe-Ge bonds.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Based on atomic position considerations, we suggest that the shorter Fe-Te nearest neighbor distance (2.63 \AA ) in \FGT with respect to \FethGT (2.643 \AA) possibly favors the inter-slab exchange mediated by the Te sites (highlighted by the curved line in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (j)).}
elements.sent-2411-089: Overall, we speculate that the inter-slab exchange interaction revealed by our study could be crucial for determining the high $T_{\rm C}$ in \FGT, endorsed by recent studies on epitaxially grown thin films where $T_{\rm C}$ is found to be inversely proportional to the inter-slab distance~\cite{SilinskasOnFilms .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figures/fig3.pdf} \caption{Investigation of charge order in \FGT by \gls*{REXS} and \gls*{XRD}. (a) A sketch highlighting the measured symmetry equivalent $\bm{Q}$ positions. (b) $H$, $K$, and $L$ scans of a representative (1/3 1/3 7.5) peak using 2.1 keV x-ray energy at 150 K. (c) The temperature dependence of the (1/3 1/3 7.5) peak. (d) Fixed-energy $\bm{Q}$-scan of (1/3 1/3 $L$) (green dotted line), (2/3 -1/3 $L$) (pink dotted line), and of (0 0 $L$) (gray line) along the $L$ direction. The temperature was set to 7 K for this measurement and the x-ray energy was tuned prior to the Fe $K - $edge, $\sim$ 7.1 keV. Double peaks in (0 0 $L$) scan are due to a second crystallite. (0 0 $L$) scan is rescaled to match y-scale. (e) Absolute intensity comparison between (1/3 1/3 22.5) and (0 0 9) peaks. (f) Fixed-$\bm{Q}$ energy scan of (1/3 1/3 22.5) (green dotted line) and (0 0 9) (gray dotted line) peaks, rescaled to match y-scale. The Fe $K - $edge \gls*{XAS} of \FGT measured in total fluoresce yield (TFY) mode is displayed (rescaled) for reference (blue dotted line).}\label{FIG:charge_order}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Absence of charge order in bulk \FGT
elements.sent-2411-090: Recently, selected area electron diffraction studies \cite{Gao2020SpontaneousFe5xGeTe2 reported a superstructure modulation vector $\bm{Q _1$= $\pm $1/3 (1 1 3) above 100 K, fading away at lower temperatures with a diffused line along 1/3 (1 1 $L$) together with a new $\bm{Q _2$ = $\pm $3/10 (0 0 3$L$) order.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: A short range $\sqrt{3 a \times \sqrt{3 a$ $R 30^\circ$ order was observed by single crystal \gls*{XRD and the high-angle annular dark field image and associated with Fe occupancy of a crystallographic split-site \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Further STM and ARPES investigations \cite{Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 confirmed the existence of a $\sqrt{3 a \times \sqrt{3 a$ $R 30^\circ$ in-plane periodic modulation and assigned it to charge ordering.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: To further the understanding of the bulk signature, electronic character and propagation vectors of $\bm{Q _1$ and $\bm{Q _2$ in \FGT, we present synchrotron \gls*{XRD ($\sim$ 2.1 keV) and \gls*{REXS (Fe $K - $edge, $\sim$ 7.1 keV) investigations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We confirmed the in-plane order $\sqrt{3 a \times \sqrt{3 a$ $R 30^\circ$ in our \FGT single crystal by finding several symmetry-equivalent peaks at 150 K, as represented in Fig. \ref{FIG:charge_order (a).}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Figure \ref{FIG:charge_order (b) reports the $H$, $K$, and $L$ scans of a representative peak with (1/3 1/3) in-plane components, measured using $\sim$ 2.1 keV x-rays.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Interestingly, we find that the out-of-plane component peaks at $L$ = 7.5 r.l.u.
elements.sent-2411-097: No significant temperature dependence is detected for this peak while going across 100 K, as displayed in Fig. \ref{FIG:charge_order (c).}
elements.sent-2411-098: The extended $L$ scan of (2/3 -1/3 $L$) and (1/3 1/3 $L$) peaks are presented in Fig. \ref{FIG:charge_order (d), using $\sim$ 7.1 keV x-rays (dotted lines): a clear (1/3 1/3 3$n$+1.5) with $n$ = 0, 1, 2, 3....}
elements.sent-2411-099: The observed (1/3 1/3 3$n$+1.5) modulation differs from the previously reported $\pm $1/3 (1 1 3) order \cite{Gao2020SpontaneousFe5xGeTe2 and it is also distinct from the (0 0 3$n$) selection rule expected for a rhombohedral unit cell (space group $R\bar{3 m$).}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For reference, we reproduce in Fig.~\ref{FIG:charge_order (d) the (0 0 $L$) scan (gray line) showing the (0 0 3$n$) periodicity of the structural Bragg peak.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The satellite structure visible in the (0 0 $L$) scan is attributed to a second crystallite present in the sample.
elements.sent-2411-102: Also no fingerprint of $\bm{Q _2$ = $\pm $3/10(0 0 3$L$) is observed in our data down to 7 K.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The intensities of (0 0 $L$) and (2/3 -1/3 $L$) or (1/3 1/3 $L$) scans cannot be directly compared in Fig.~\ref{FIG:charge_order (d), as we used a higher intensity detuning filter for the main structural Bragg peaks along (0 0 $L$).}
elements.sent-2411-104: An absolute intensity comparison between (1/3 1/3 22.5) and (0 0 9) is presented in \ref{FIG:charge_order (d), showing that the intensities of both peaks are of the same order of magnitude, with the (0 0 9) peak approximately 50\% stronger than (1/3 1/3 22.5).}
elements.sent-2411-105: Additionally, using Fe $K - $edge \gls*{REXS , we investigated the resonance effect of the (1/3 1/3 22.5) peak.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The resulting fixed-$\bm{Q}$ energy scan is presented in Fig. \ref{FIG:charge_order (e) (green dotted line), and compared to the energy scan of the (0 0 9) structural Bragg peak (gray line).}
elements.sent-2411-107: Both scans present a similar behavior, with no resonance enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-108: The Fe $K - $edge XAS of \FGT is reproduced for reference in the same figure.
elements.sent-2411-109: Overall, our \gls*{REXS and \gls*{XRD studies report the presence of a commensurate (1/3 1/3 $L$) Bragg peak with a 3$n$+1.5 out-of-plane modulation, no temperature dependence down to 7 K, and no resonance effect.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Based on these observations, we propose these peaks to be of structural origin rather than electronic \,\cite{Abbamonte2004CrystallizationSr14Cu24O41,Chen2016Remarkable4,Shen2021ChargeNickelates, Shan2023chargeorder .}
elements.sent-2411-111: This conclusion is further corroborated by the comparable peak intensity with respect to main structural Bragg peaks.
elements.sent-2411-112: Furthermore, we underline that the observed 3$n$+1.5 out-of-plane periodicity is not compatible with the space group $R\bar{3 m$ proposed for \FGT, instead, it infers doubling of structural unit cell along the $\bm{c}$-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-113: To reconcile the discrepancy between our observations and the ones reported in the literature \cite{Gao2020SpontaneousFe5xGeTe2, Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 , we should acknowledge intrinsic differences connected with the techniques involved.}
elements.sent-2411-114: STM and ARPES mostly probe the surface, which in certain materials can significantly differ from the bulk \cite{Brown2005SurfaceTransition, Gu2023DetectionUTe2, Kengle2024Absence2,Theuss2024Absence2 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Electron diffraction instead probes the bulk, but the required sample preparation methods - such as focused ion beam and grinding - might introduce permanent micro-structural changes affecting the material response \cite{Mayer2007TEMDamage, Volkert2007FocusedMicromachining .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Finally, we cannot exclude a sample to sample variation in determining the ultimate material properties.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our work sheds light on the magnetic excitations and electronic order in the room temperature vdW ferromagnet \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-118: Using high-resolution RIXS at the Fe $L_3 - $edge, we reveal that \FGT hosts dual character of magnetic excitations composed of a magnon and a continuum, similarly to the more extensively characterized \FethGT, and prove that the dual character of magnetic excitation driven by orbital-selective Mott transition is common to ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials.
elements.sent-2411-119: The out-of-plane modulation of the continuum intensity reveals the presence of a strong out-of-plane inter-slab exchange path, where the interacting magnetic units are the neighboring \FeGeTe slabs.
elements.sent-2411-120: We conclude that \FGT behaves like a 3D magnet, and we discuss this finding in connection to its $T_{\rm C}$, the highest among \FeGeTe vdW materials.
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, employing \gls*{XRD and \gls*{REXS , we explored the bulk character, the electronic origin and the propagation vector of the previously reported charge order with (1/3 1/3) in-plane components.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The identified $\pm$(1/3 1/3 1.5) propagation vector requires doubling of the crystallographic unit cell.

elements.subsection-2411-004: X-ray Absorption (XAS) and resonant inelastic x-ray Scattering (RIXS)
elements.sent-2411-137: High-resolution \gls*{XAS and \gls*{RIXS experiments were carried out at the SIX 2-ID beamline of NSLS-II ~\cite{DovrakRSI2016 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The experimental energy resolution at the Fe $L_3 - $edge ($\sim$ 708 eV) was 21 meV for the RIXS measurements, determined by the full width at half-maximum of the elastic peak measured from a reference multilayer sample.
elements.sent-2411-139: To suppress the elastic line, $\pi-$ polarization was used throughout the experiment.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Resonant elastic x-ray scattering (REXS) and x-ray diffraction (XRD)
elements.sent-2411-140: Synchrotron based \gls*{XRD was used to measure the rocking curves and temperature dependence of (1/3 1/3 7.5) peak at 29-ID beamline of APS, with a photon energy of 2.1keV.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The Fe $K - $edge \gls*{REXS was performed at the 4-ID beamline of NSLS-II.}
elements.sent-2411-142: The Raman measurements were taken using a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman microscope from Horiba Scientific using 100 $\times$ 0.9NA Olympus air objective.
elements.sent-2411-143: Spectra were recorded using an 1800 gr/mm holographic grating and Synapse II front-illuminated EMCCD.
elements.sent-2411-144: We performed room-temperature Raman scattering ($\lambda$ = 6328 \AA) on the exfoliated flake (oriented as $c$-axis normal) to determine phonons in \FfGT, see Fig. \ref{FIG:phonons .}
elements.sent-2411-145: For these measurements, we exfoliated a \FfGT flake on SiO$_2$/Si.
elements.sent-2411-146: The strongest phonon is observed below $<$ 150 cm$^{-1}$ (20 meV) i.e. no overlap with magnon or continuum.
elements.sent-2411-147: We assume 20 meV phonon to have the dominant contribution in the \gls*{RIXS spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Raman lines around 62 meV originates from Si/SiO$_2$ substrate.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{figures/Phonon.pdf} \caption{Raman scattering from an exfoliated flake. The contribution from phonons to the spectrum is $\leq 20$ meV, highlighted by yellow color region.}\label{FIG:phonons}
elements.section-2411-007: Self-absorption correction to the RIXS intensity
elements.sent-2411-149: Outgoing photons with energy close to the absorption/resonance edge get considerably reabsorbed.
elements.sent-2411-150: Moreover, the path length traveled or the time spent by the photons inside the sample depends on the scattering geometry.
elements.sent-2411-151: Both effects combined affect the measured RIXS intensity, and when comparing absolute signal of two or more scattering geometries, it is essential to consider these aspects.
elements.sent-2411-152: The raw RIXS intensity can be corrected, to the first approximation, for these effects with information of X-ray absorption measured in total electron yield (TEY) and the corresponding scattering geometry \cite{Wang_Saturation_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-153: RIXS data shown in the main body of the paper was corrected for self-absorption effects following the procedure used in Refs.~\cite{Wang_Saturation_2020,Robarts2021DynamicalScattering .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Only scattering geometry lead self-absorption effects are considered as no dichroism is observed in TEY -- similar absorption profile for $\sigma$ and $\pi$- polarizations.
elements.sent-2411-155: The corrected RIXS intensity $I_c$ is given as following: \begin{equation}I_{c} = I_{m} \circledast \mathcal{A}\big(\theta,\Omega,\mu(\omega_{in}),\mu(\omega_{out})\big)\end{equation} where $ I_{m}$ is measured RIXS intensity and the self-absorption correcting factor $\mathcal{A}$ is defined as, \begin{equation}\mathcal{A} = \frac{\mu(\omega_{in})\sin{(\Omega-\theta)} + \mu(\omega_{out}) \sin\theta}{\sin{(\Omega-\theta)}}\end{equation} $\mathcal{A}$ is a function of scattering geometry ($\Omega$ is angle between incoming and outgoing photon and $\theta$ is photon incidence angle measured from the sample surface as shown in Fig. 1(a), TEY, and, incoming and outgoing photon energy, $\mu(\omega_{in )$ is the total electron-yield (TEY) maximum (at Fe $L_3$- resonance) and $\mu(\omega_{out )$ is TEY value at the corresponding $\omega_{out}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: TEY was measured in the same ($\theta-\Omega$) scattering geometry as RIXS.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{figures/SingleRIXS.pdf} \caption{A representative raw RIXS spectrum. The florescence peak around 2 eV is a signature of metallic system. Its low energy part containing magnetic excitation (shown in Fig. 1(e)) is used for modeling.}\label{FIG:SingleRIXS}
elements.sent-2411-157: In this section we describe the fitting procedure of the self-absorption corrected low-energy portion [0 $-$ 300] meV RIXS intensity.
elements.sent-2411-158: A complete RIXS scan is shown in Fig. \ref{FIG:SingleRIXS .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The elastic line was modeled by a Pseudo-Voigt model: \begin{equation}\begin{split} f_1(x;A,\mu,\sigma,\alpha) = \frac{(1-\alpha)A}{\sigma_g \sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma_g^2} + \frac{\alpha A}{\pi} \frac{\sigma}{(x-\mu)^2+\sigma^2} \end{split}\end{equation} and the phonon and magnon by a Gaussian distribution: \begin{equation}f_2(x;A,\mu,\sigma) = \frac{A}{\sigma \sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{[-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2]}\end{equation} while to accommodate the damped and broad character of the continumm, it was modeled by a Skewed Gaussian: \begin{equation}f_3(x;A,\mu,\sigma,\gamma) = \frac{A}{\sigma \sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{[-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2]} \{1+\text{erf}[\frac{\gamma(x-\mu)}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}] \}\end{equation} The fluorescence tail background was modeled by a step function broadened by the twice experimental resolution: \begin{equation}f_4(x,A_0,A,\mu,\sigma) = A[1+\text{erf}((x-\mu)/\sigma)]/2\end{equation} Here $A$ is the amplitude, $\mu$ is the center, $\sigma$ is the standard-deviation, $\alpha$ is the fraction, Final model describing the entire RIXS spectrum for $E < 300$ meV.
elements.sent-2411-160: As a result, function capturing the low energy RIXS spectrum is as follows -- \begin{equation}\begin{split} I = \text{Elastic}(f_1) + \text{Phonon} (f_2) + \text{Magnon} (f_2) + \text{Continuum} (f_3) + \text{Background} (f_4) \end{split}\end{equation} During fitting of all the \gls*{RIXS spectra vs $\bm{Q}$, the following procedure was adopted.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} I = Elastic(f_1) + Phonon (f_2) + Magnon (f_2) + Continuum (f_3) + Background (f_4) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-161: Elastic line: $ \sigma, \alpha$ was kept fixed and only amplitude $A$ and $\mu$ were allowed to change.
elements.sent-2411-162: Phonon: $\sigma$ was fixed by experimental resolution and $\mu$ was fixed to 20 meV from the Raman measurements and amplitude $A$ was allowed to change as $\sin^2(\pi L)$ \cite{Lin2070StronglyAnomaly for in-plane scans, however, it was fixed for out-of-plane dependence.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Magnon: Both $A$ and $\mu$ were allowed to change, but $\sigma$ was fixed to a higher value than the resolution to account for continuum contamination, for out-of-plane scans, both $A$ and $\mu$ were fixed.
elements.sent-2411-164: Continuum: Both its position $\mu$ and width $\sigma$ and $\gamma$ were fixed, however, its amplitude $A$ was allowed to change.
elements.sent-2411-166: Understanding the ground state of van der Waals (vdW) magnets is crucial for designing devices leveraging these platforms.
elements.sent-2411-167: Here, we investigate the magnetic excitations and charge order in \FGT, a vdW ferromagnet with $\approx$ 315 K Curie temperature.
elements.sent-2411-168: Using Fe $L_3 - $edge resonant inelastic x-ray scattering, we observe a dual nature of magnetic excitations, comprising a coherent magnon and a broad non-dispersive continuum extending up to 150 meV, 50$\%$ higher than in \FetsGT.
elements.sent-2411-169: The continuum intensity is sinusoidally modulated along the stacking direction $L$, with a period matching the inter-slab distance.
elements.sent-2411-170: Our results indicate that while the dual character of the magnetic excitations is generic to Fe-Ge-Te vdW magnets, \FGT exhibits a longer out-of-plane magnetic correlation length, suggesting enhanced 3D magnetic character.
elements.sent-2411-171: Furthermore, resonant x-ray diffraction reveals that previously reported $ \pm$(1/3, 1/3, $L$) peaks originate from crystal structure rather than from charge order.
elements.sent-2411-172: Introduction The discovery of ferromagnetism in two-dimensional van der Waals (vdW) ferromagnets have opened exciting frontiers in quantum matter research \cite{Burch2018MagnetismMaterials,Gibertini2019MagneticHeterostructures,Wang2021TheMaterials .}
elements.sent-2411-173: In this context, the ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials, such as \FethGT and \FfGT , hosting high-temperature long-range ferromagnetic order down to the \gls*{2D structural limit is a unique platform where miniaturized spintronics devices have already been proposed \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle,Zhang2020ItinerantTemperature,Chen2023Above-Room-Temperature2,Wang2018TunnelingHeterostructures,Georgopoulou-Kotsaki2023Significant,Li2018Patterning,Alghamdi2019Highly,Yang2020Highly,Ding2020Observtion,Wu2020Neel,Deng2018Gate-tunableFe3GeTe2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: In addition, strong electronic correlations are reported to drive these systems towards exotic ground states, that is, the heavy Fermions/ Kondo physics~\cite{Yun2018Emergence,Bao2022Neutron2 , \gls*{CO ~\cite{Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 , flat bands \cite{Wu2023ReversibleFerromagnet , optical anomalous hall effect (AHE) \cite{Kato_npj2022 , and \gls*{OSMT ~\cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: Are these dual magnetic excitations, a generic feature of the ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials? If so, how \vb{do these excitations evolve with $T_{\rm C}$? \FGT with the highest $T_{\rm{C \approx$ 315 K among \FeGeTe 2D-vdW ferromagnetic materials is an ideal member to investigate these questions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: However, despite enormous progress in understanding transport, magnetic and structural properties of \FfxGT \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle,Zhang2020ItinerantTemperature,Chen2023Above-Room-Temperature2 , to date, the character of the magnetic excitations, their energy scale, and dimensionality remain unexplored in this material.}
elements.sent-2411-177: The \gls*{INS is a standard technique to address these issues \cite{Bettler2019MagneticPbVO,Bhartiya2021InelasticBaVO but the small size of the \FGT single crystals poses a challenge.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: Here we present an investigation of the magnetic excitations and charge order in \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-179: To elucidate the character, energy scale, and dimensionality of the magnetic excitations, we employed high-resolution Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS on a mm sized single crystal \FGT.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Our findings suggests that \FGT hosts dual character of magnetic excitations, similarly to \FetsGT, and despite being a \gls*{2D system structurally, it displays a significant three-dimensional (3D) interaction between \FeGeTe slabs that are $\sim$10 \AA \, apart.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figures/fig1.pdf} \caption{Magnetic excitations in the Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS} spectra of \FGT. (a) \FGT crystal structure drawn using VESTA \cite{VESTA}. A single unit cell contains three \FeGeTe slabs. (b) Schematic of \gls*{RIXS} experimental geometry. In the hexagonal structure representation, two orthogonal wave vectors -- (100) and (001) -- form the scattering plane highlighted in pink. $\theta$ is an angle between the incident x-ray and the (100) axis while $\Omega$ is the scattering angle between incoming and outgoing x-ray. The double headed arrow represents the $\pi$ polarization of the incident light. (c) Fe $L_{3, 2} - $edge XAS measured at $\theta = 15^\circ$ in total electron yield (TEY) mode at 70 K. A solid vertical line represents the Fe $L_3 - $edge maximum, that corresponds to the incident photon energy (707.9 eV) used for the \gls*{RIXS} spectra. (d) RIXS intensity map measured as a function of incident photon around the Fe $L_3 - $edge, and zoomed in to cover excitations below 300 meV. For this dataset, $\theta = 20^\circ$ and $\Omega = 95^\circ$ corresponding to $\bm{Q}$ = ($-0.16$ 0 2.17). (e-f). Two representative \gls*{RIXS} scans (black dotted line) at ($-0.35$ 0 1.4) and ($-0.3$ 0 1.74). The low-energy excitations are fitted with an elastic peak (gray), a phonon (blue), a magnon (red), a broad continuum, and a background (gray line) stretching to higher energies. The red solid line represents the fit sum. The inset in (e) shows the measured \FfGT single crystal. All RIXS spectra displayed in this work were measured at $T$ = 70 K, using $\pi$ polarization. The error bars of the \gls*{RIXS} spectra are defined assuming a Poisson distribution of the single-photon counted events.}\label{FIG:fig1}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig2.pdf} \caption{$L$ -- dependent study of the magnetic continuum in \FGT. (a-g) \gls*{RIXS} spectra measured at different $L$ values from 1.74 r.l.u. to 3.23 r.l.u., at 70 K. For all spectra, $H < |0.04|$ and $K$= 0. Each spectrum is fitted with an elastic peak (gray), a phonon (blue), a magnon (red), and a continuum (green), as introduced in Figs. \ref{FIG:fig1}(e, f). The vertical dashed line marks the continuum centers around $\sim$ 75 meV. (h) $L$-dependence of the continuum integrated spectral weight (green dotted line). The error-bars are half of one standard deviation. The thick green line is a sinusoidal fit of the data using Eq. (\ref{eq:equation}) for $d$ = 10 \AA, while the dotted lines represent the upper and lower boundaries considering the extracted fit error for $d$ of $\pm$ 1 \AA. (i) Calculated intensity modulation for intra-slab (red) and inter-slab (black) distances, as defined within the crystal structure displayed in (j) using with the same color convention. A gold curved line in (j) highlights the Te -- Te distance across two neighboring slabs.} \label{FIG:ldep}
elements.sent-2411-181: The high-quality quenched single crystal of Fe$_{4.75}$GeTe$_2$ used for this study originated from the same batch as in Ref.~\cite{MayPRB2016_Fe3GT,May2019FerromagnetismArticle , which suggested similar magnetic properties for nominal composition ranging from Fe$_{4.7}$GeTe$_2$ to Fe$_{6.0}$GeTe$_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Figure~\ref{FIG:fig1 (a) shows the rhombohedral unit cell of \FGT ($R\bar{3 m$ No. 166, a = b $\approx $ 4.04 \AA, \, c $\approx$ 29.19 \AA), characterized by three distinct \FeGeTe slabs separated by $\approx$10 \AA.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: It develops ferromagnetic order below $T_{\rm{C \approx$ 315 K with magnetic moment pointing along the $c-$axis~\cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle .}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: The observed $L_3$ and $L_2$ absorption peaks are in agreement with literature \cite{Yamagami2022EnhancedFe5GT , supporting the high quality of the sample.}
elements.sent-2411-185: The \gls*{XAS data was used to identify the resonant energy for the \gls*{RIXS study.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Extensive momentum coverage was achieved for the \gls*{XRD experiment (2 keV of photon energy) and for the Fe $K - $edge \gls*{REXS ( $\approx$ 7 keV of photon energy).}}
elements.sent-2411-187: For Fe $L_3 - $edge \gls*{RIXS ($\approx$ 710 eV of photon energy), we could span a large portion of the $\bm{Q}$-space in the $L$ direction across several \gls*{BZ and up to $L$ = 3.23 $(2\pi/c)$, thanks to the large $c$-axis ($\approx$ 29.19 \AA) of the \FGT, while in-plane we could cover up to $H_{max = -0.35$ $(4\pi/\sqrt{3 a)$ within the first BZ.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: In the following two sections we describe the investigation of the magnetic excitations and their momentum dependence in \FGT using Fe $L_3 - $edge RIXS, and we discuss the results in comparison \FetsGT \cite{Calder2019,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2, Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Although most of the spectral weight goes into high-energy fluorescence as expected for a metal (see Supplementary Figure 2) \cite{Pelliciari2021TuningConfinement , a weak component stretches from 0 up to $\sim$ 150 meV resonating at the Fe $L_3$ peak.}
elements.sent-2411-190: Details regarding the fitting and its procedure are reported in Supplementary Note 3.
elements.sent-2411-191: Taking \FetsGT as a reference case \cite{Bao2022Neutron2,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 , we include in the fitting model a resolution limited phonon (blue peak), a magnon mode (red peak) due to spin-spin correlations between localized spins, and a broad magnetic continuum (green peak) coming from spin-spin interactions among itinerant electrons.}
elements.sent-2411-192: We fix the phonon energy to 20 meV based on the room temperature Raman measurements presented in Supplementary Figure 1 and consistently with \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: From the remaining spectral weight, we extract a magnon peak centered at 36 meV $\pm 1$~meV and a continuum centered at 75 meV $\pm 2$~meV, extending up to $\approx$ 150 meV.
elements.sent-2411-194: In our dataset, the continuum amplitude is comparable to the magnon, and its area is significantly larger than the latter, ruling out the double magnon character.
elements.sent-2411-195: While we cannot exclude the presence of double magnon in our data, their faint spectral weight would not impact the interpretation nor the extraction of the magnetic continuum.
elements.sent-2411-196: However, a comparable magnon energy for similar momentum transfer has been reported for \FetsGT \cite{Calder2019, Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2, Bao2022Neutron2 , further supporting the nature of the $\approx$ 36 meV peak.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Based on these findings, we identify the low energy magnetic excitations in \FGT, and assess the coexistence of the dual electron spin character in this system.
elements.sent-2411-198: Our conclusion is underpinned by the \gls*{INS studies of \FetsGT \cite{Bao2022Neutron2,Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-199: To explain this uncommon behavior in a 3$d$ transition metal \cite{Xie_PRL_2018,Chen_NatComm_2020,Song_npjQM_2021 , an orbital selective Mott transition mechanism has been proposed for \FetsGT \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 , typically invoked in the context of strong multi-orbital correlations as realized by 4$d$/5$d$ and $f$ electron compounds \cite{Anisimov2002,Kim_QM_2022, Medici_PRL_2005,Medici_PRL_2009 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Within this scenario, it is possible to explain both the itinerant and the local magnetism manifesting in a multi-orbital system \cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Considering the emergence of giant Fe(3$d$)$-$Te(5$p$) and Fe(3$d$)$-$Ge(4$s/4d$) hybridization \cite{Yamagami2021giant and the flat bands \cite{Wu2023ReversibleFerromagnet,Wang_PRB_2023 , and the dual character of magnetic excitations evidenced by our study, the magnetic ground state of \FGT is likely governed by \gls*{OSMT physics as well as in \FetsGT.}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Theoretical validation would be valuable to confirm this aspect, requiring a comprehensive treatment of the strong multi-orbital correlations manifesting within such a complex crystal structure.
elements.sent-2411-203: The dispersionless nature of the continuum agrees with the \FetsGT findings \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: To directly compare the absolute \gls*{RIXS intensities as a function of $L$ (or scattering geometries), all spectra reported here are normalized to integrated fluorescence peak and self-absorption corrected as described in Supplementary Note 2.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Note that while in \FetsGT the continuum is maximized around the in-plane \gls*{BZ boundary~\cite{Bao2022Neutron2 , here we find a significant spectral weight close to the in-plane \gls*{BZ center.}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: The origin of the $L$-dependent intensity modulation of the continuum in \FetsGT was associated with the two Fe sites, yielding a characteristic length scale along the $c-$axis within \FeGeTe slab \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-207: This approach enabled us to utilize a consistent fitting model across all RIXS spectra.
elements.sent-2411-208: More details on the fitting procedure can be found in Supplementary Note 3.
elements.sent-2411-209: A comparison of the $L$ - dependent magnetic continuum intensity with and without self-absorption correction is shown in Supplementary Figure 3.
elements.sent-2411-210: The magnitude of intensity modulation is robust and independent of self-absorption correction.
elements.sent-2411-211: In the case of \FetsGT, the $L$-dependent INS structure factor of the continuum yielded an intra-slab distance ${d \sim 2.7$ \AA \, \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-212: This finding suggests that the magnetic continuum in \FetsGT originates from the quasi-molecular character of the magnetic states formed by the Fe-Fe dumbbell, and furthermore it identifies the atoms and exchange path that contribute to the strongest magnetic exchange interaction in the system~\cite{Bai2022AntiferromagnetictextGeTe_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: The intra-slab distance $d \sim 2.53$ \AA\, connecting two Fe atoms within the same \FeGeTe slab fails in reproducing the observed modulation, despite it well described the $L$-dependent continuum modulation for \FetsGT \cite{Bao2022Neutron2 .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Based on atomic position considerations, we suggest that the shorter Fe-Te nearest neighbor distance (2.63 \AA ) in \FGT with respect to \FetsGT (2.643 \AA) possibly favors the inter-slab exchange mediated by the Te sites (highlighted by the curved line in Fig. \ref{FIG:ldep (j)).}
elements.sent-2411-215: Further STM and ARPES investigations \cite{Wu2021DirectGeTe_2 confirmed the existence of a $\sqrt{3 a \times \sqrt{3 a$ $R 30^\circ$ in-plane periodic modulation and assigned it to the charge ordering.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: To further the understanding of the bulk signature, electronic character and propagation vectors of $\bm{Q _1$ and $\bm{Q _2$ in \FGT, in the following, we present synchrotron \gls*{XRD ($\sim$ 2.1 keV) and \gls*{REXS (Fe $K - $edge, $\sim$ 7.1 keV) investigations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: An absolute intensity comparison between (1/3 1/3 22.5) and (0 0 9) is presented in \ref{FIG:charge_order (\vb{e ), showing that the intensities of both peaks are of the same order of magnitude, with the (0 0 9) peak approximately 50\% stronger than (1/3 1/3 22.5).}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Based on these observations, we propose these peaks originate from the crystal structure rather than charge order \, \cite{Abbamonte2004CrystallizationSr14Cu24O41,Chen2016Remarkable4,Shen2021ChargeNickelates, Shan2023chargeorder .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Furthermore, we underline that the observed 3$n$+1.5 out-of-plane periodicity is not compatible with the space group $R\bar{3 m$ proposed for \FGT, instead, it infers doubling of structural unit cell along the $\bm{c}$-axis and reduced crystal symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-220: One direct consequence of reduced lattice symmetry would be a more complex network of magnetic interactions, which might be crucial to interpret the various kinks in the magnetization versus temperature diagram \cite{May2019FerromagnetismArticle .}
elements.sent-2411-221: To advance the understanding of this complex system, it would be helpful to perform on the same sample both surface sensitive (STM, ARPES) and bulk sensitive (XRD, REXS) investigations.
elements.sent-2411-222: In summary, our work sheds light on the magnetic excitations and electronic order in the room temperature vdW ferromagnet \FGT.
elements.sent-2411-223: Using high-resolution RIXS at the Fe $L_3 - $edge, we reveal that \FGT hosts dual character of magnetic excitations composed of a magnon and a continuum, similarly to the more extensively characterized \FetsGT, and prove that the dual character of magnetic excitation, known to be driven by orbital selective Mott transition, is common to ternary \FeGeTe vdW materials.
elements.sent-2411-224: The absence of temperature and resonance effects, and the commensurability with the crystal structure led us to conclude that, the observed Bragg peaks do not originate from charge order but rather crystal structure with reduced symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-225: The presence of the (1/3 1/3 3$n$+1.5) peaks suggests a doubling of the unit cell along the c-axis, indicating a subtle change in the stacking sequence or atomic positions that is not captured by the $R\bar{3 m$ space group.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Synchrotron based \gls*{XRD was used to measure the rocking curves and temperature dependence of (1/3 1/3 7.5) peak at 29-ID beamline of APS, with a photon energy of 2.1 keV.}
elements.sent-2411-227: The Fe $K-$ edge \gls*{REXS was performed at the 4-ID beamline of NSLS-II.}
elements.sent-2411-228: We performed room-temperature Raman scattering ($\lambda$ = 6328 \AA) on a \FGT flake exfoliated on SiO$_2$/Si and oriented as $c$-axis normal using a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman microscope from Horiba Scientific using 100 $\times$ 0.9NA Olympus air objective.
elements.sent-2411-229: Spectra were recorded using an 1800 gr/mm holographic grating and Synapse II front-illuminated EMCCD (Supplementary Figure 1).
elements.sent-2411-230: Data supporting the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
elements.sent-2411-231: The authors declare no competing interests.
elements.sent-2411-232: The strongest phonon, at room temperature, is observed below $<$ 150 cm$^{-1}$ (20 meV) i.e. no overlap with magnon or continuum (Supplementary Figure \ref{FIG:phonons ).}
elements.sent-2411-233: The corrected RIXS intensity $I_c$ is given as following: \begin{equation}I_{c} = I_{m} \circledast \mathcal{A}\big(\theta,\Omega,\mu(\omega_{in}),\mu(\omega_{out})\big)\end{equation} where $ I_{m}$ is measured RIXS intensity and the self-absorption correcting factor $\mathcal{A}$ is defined as, \begin{equation}\mathcal{A} = \frac{\mu(\omega_{in})\sin{(\Omega-\theta)} + \mu(\omega_{out}) \sin\theta}{\sin{(\Omega-\theta)}}\end{equation} $\mathcal{A}$ is a function of scattering geometry ($\Omega$ is angle between incoming and outgoing photon and $\theta$ is photon incidence angle measured from the sample surface as shown in Fig. 1(a) of the main text, TEY, and, incoming and outgoing photon energy, $\mu(\omega_{in )$ is the total electron-yield (TEY) maximum (at Fe $L_3$- resonance) and $\mu(\omega_{out )$ is TEY value at the corresponding $\omega_{out}$.}}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{figures/SingleRIXS.pdf} \caption{A representative raw RIXS spectrum. The fluorescence peak around 2 eV is a signature of metallic system. Its low energy part containing magnetic excitation (shown in Fig. 1(e) of the main text) is used for modeling.}\label{FIG:SingleRIXS}
elements.sent-2411-234: A complete RIXS scan is shown in Supplementary Figure\ref{FIG:SingleRIXS .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Phonon: $\sigma$ was fixed by experimental resolution and $\mu$ was fixed to 20 meV from the Raman measurements and amplitude $A$ was allowed to change as $\sin^2(\pi L)$ \cite{Lin2070StronglyAnomaly for in-plane scans, however, it was fixed to \vbb{$L = 3.23$} scan out-of-plane dependence.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Magnon: Both $A$ and $\mu$ were allowed to change, but $\sigma$ was fixed to \vbb{30\% higher value than the resolution to account for continuum contamination \vbb{for in-plane scans , and for the out-of-plane scans, both $A$ and $\mu$ were fixed to \vbb{$L = 3.23$}.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Continuum: Both its width $\sigma$ and $\gamma$ were fixed, however, its amplitude $A$ and $\mu$ were allowed to change.
elements.section-2411-012: Continuum intensity modulation with and without self-absorption correction
elements.sent-2411-238: A comparison of robust magnetic continuum intensity modulation between raw and self-absorption corrected RIXS spectrum is shown in Supplementary Figure \ref{FIG:comparison .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{figures/lmod.pdf} \caption{\vb{A comparison of the magnetic continuum intensity modulation. The scale of intensity variation is not significantly affected by the self-absorption correction. The raw RIXS spectra (blue) and self-absorption corrected spectra (orange) evolve in similar fashion, demonstrating $\approx$ 300 \% evolution. }} \label{FIG:comparison}
